speaker: interviewer
text: You were just telling me that after you had your first encounter with some kinder Russian soldiers, you changed groups, and you joined a partisan group that had a name, which you can't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember their name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the men in that group had some knifes. Could you tell us more about what you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the knifes-- the men said they went out looking for food. And they went in in a place where they were soldiered there, and they left there. And when they came in, they find lots of knifes. And they find potatoes. He took the potatoes and the knifes, and he brought stones. And that's the way we used to stay all night and all day, sharpening the knives. And they said, that going to help us if somebody came. In as we were talking to fight with the German. And I had a knife, too. And I said that knife is going to be for my father, [INAUDIBLE] he killed for my mother. I didn't know my mother's alive. I didn't know if I had family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I was eight years.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were eight years old and you were living under the assumption that you had no family alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how that felt for you. What you would think about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very depressed. Too scared to talk. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was scared to tell, give me a little bit water, too. They're going to threw me out. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody comfort you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time was not comfort. [SOBBING] Everybody was self.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep yourself going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to close my eyes and [INAUDIBLE] said, [SOBBING] someday I'm going to find my mom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you think about? [SOB] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people didn't like it when when another child-- when there's a shortage in food. When in-- in a war time, you don't get human. You get like an animal. You find something, you hide it for yourself. You don't think for somebody else. And they were strange people. If they had a piece-- something, they find it. I was very [? fast. ?] So they-- some-- some people ate raw meat-- horse meat. And the horses was running. And the electric fall down. And they were [? electricute. ?] And that's where they took. They cut it off meat. And they ate raw meat. I couldn't eat it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I tried to chew that, and I [INAUDIBLE]. I couldn't eat it. We went out. We used to take grass, you know, leaves. Special leaves, what that one lady said, that the bears eating them. And we used to take the leaves and chew on them to get a little bit moisture in the mouth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at once, I wished myself falling in the German. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] They was talking about concentration camps, and I listened, and I said, it's not far here, a concentration camp? They said, no, it's not here. We have to go-- in our city was not concentration camp. The only thing I was wishing to be in a concentration camp is better what to be underground. Tell me more about what was so difficult underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Condition. We didn't have a toilet. You know. We didn't have nothing. You-- you were sitting with open legs, and one was sitting besides you. And another one. You couldn't move.

speaker: interviewer
text: The last one was always the worst time-- thing. The last one had cold weather. You know? You-- you always be with people, together, you were warm. And if you were the last one, you were really shaking. Was no-- everything was wet, cold. And the winter was colder like in the spring. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime in the morning, you wake up. And you [? think ?] if you're alive or not. You want to start talking, and you can't. You're so dry in mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any things that were more comforting or made the time pass quicker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just talking. We were just talking, not to leave. Don't-- have hope. Think tomorrow is another day. Tomorrow, maybe we will be-- have a better day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever sick? And were there medical supplies?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were never sick. Never had a cold. Not just me. I'm telling you. Everyone who was there. Some didn't have shoes. We-- well I can't forget that lady, a girl. No dress-- was ripped in pieces. And was not-- was nothing to cover you. And in the winter, she walked the same way everybody needed to go in the nighttime to help. And she never-- she said, I like to go two time. Maybe I catch a cold, and I be dying. But nobody-- the only thing people died is from hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody die in your group underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, yeah. A boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just talking there. People were talking. Maybe we're going to have to eat people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that make you feel hearing those words?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't understand that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to his body?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the nighttime, they took-- they took him out, and they bury him there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They put something-- they said [INAUDIBLE] when I'm a survivor, I'm going to come, you know. But there-- there was lots of people we would not cover. They were just dead people. Just-- I don't think animals with so many dead, like, people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say Kaddish, or were there any Jewish rituals maintained while you were underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they said-- they were said-- that they always said something. They said Kaddish and they-- that's all what we-- they could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep any other observances? Or did you mark the days and know when it was Shabbat, and--?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody, I didn't think that they knew what date we have. We didn't know when is night, when is daytime. We needed to open it to see when. We didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you keep track of the days, the months?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next? You were with that group for a while, and then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next group [INAUDIBLE] you know, another partisan. And they didn't want to take small kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told us the best thing, you stay here. The Russian coming. That's the place where the Russian went to the front, you know. And we were stay-- we stayed there. We were there, [? but ?] 20 kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: All by yourselves? No adult?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no adults. And the Russian-- the first [? tank ?] came didn't want to stop. The second didn't stop. [INAUDIBLE] Maybe five, six later they stopped. And they just start throw to us clothing and food. And we were running after them. And we came with them. When they stopped, we stopped. And they gave us food. We came to Uzbekistan.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year is? I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how old you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't remember my age. [LAUGHS] I think I was-- I think I was 8, 8 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So around 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that. '39 or '40. Something like that. And we came to Uzbekistan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Russian, I think. Uzbekistan is Russia, no?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They spoke not Russia. They spoke a different language, you know, just like-- like Arabs. They took us there. The soldier gave us over to a lady. And the lady took us there. And in that time, I've-- a lady recognized me. She was from our city. Her name was Yenta Barul. And she took me in to her house.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Uzbekistan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And in Uzbekistan, we didn't-- she took me in, and she put my name on your name, like she adopted me. And she'd never-- I asked her, you saw my mom? You saw somebody? She said no. Her husband was a painter. And she was not very kind to me. That time, I thought she was very nice. But she was not. She didn't send me to school. I was working there. Carried the water, helping him painting, cleaning the house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] After that time, was signing the Polish people to go back to Pole, [INAUDIBLE]. They were from the first war. We came to Poland, [? Wroclaw ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: So you moved with her and her husband? Did she have other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had two kids, a boy and a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was younger. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, your children had a mother and a father. And they-- they didn't have to work. Did nothing, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were treated very differently.

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, I thought-- I mean, I'm she's a angel. [INAUDIBLE] Later, when I looked around, you know, she was not nice to me. And in Pole, every child got lots of food, you know, the best food for kids' survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave that food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was Poland gave the survivors. Poland gave by each week a [? patch ?] like good chocolate, butter, sugar, all kind of things. And she took [? them ?] from me. I never got it. I didn't know what it mean, a piece of chocolate. I didn't know. They sold it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a Polish lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a Jewish lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: From our town, from our city. She knew my mom. And that time my brother find me. My brother got killed in Israel. My brother Meir.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one who stayed home with you to look after your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was the-- the second from me. He was the third child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and Meir found each.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meir find me. And came to me and want to take me away from that family. And they didn't want to give it to him. And he said, my name is not [NON-ENGLISH] My name is [NON-ENGLISH]. I forgot his-- [INAUDIBLE]. And he went and he stole me in the night. And he brought me in a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. In I would [INAUDIBLE] Wroclaw. And I think in it was in Wroclaw, in a kibbutz. That kibbutz-- in a week later, they send away kids to Germany. Camp [INAUDIBLE]. I was a [INAUDIBLE]. And from that time, was eight kids, the first one. Brought it in in Israel--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --underground It was in 1948. I was a-- I was in time was still the England people was in Israel. And we walked on mountains, Salzburg, all kind of places. The feet was swolen like. And we just thought we're going in our country. We're going to have our country. And they brought us over in boats. And they brought us to Kinneret. And that was the first thing. But Kinneret took us. My passport is marked I'm a born Israeli only for that. Every week, they used to come, the English people, to check in the kibbutz if somebody-- newcomers-- coming in underground. And the first kids, they brought it here, it right away was passport for them. And right away, it's marked I'm born in Kinneret. And this and this year. And-- and my name was from Henya was changed to Edna.

speaker: interviewer
text: So another identity.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Hebrew-- a Hebrew name. They didn't change different writing. Only in Hebrew Henya is a Jewish name. And in Israel, they thought they're going to find if they changed me on Edna. And at that time in kibbutz, I started going to school half a day. I went to school, and half a day I was working. And in kibbutz, the kibbutz went to find my family. Slow, slow like. Then became-- my brother came over, time from the England already. His ship was [NON-ENGLISH] ship. The day he came 63 kids came to Kinneret. He was the only one what I knew. And slow, slow, the people from the kibbutz, they find my mother. And they find my mother, then younger brother, Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my sister came, I think, in a year when-- when I find my sister when we got-- what year? In '50?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1950. That-- that the last one was me find my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were reunited with--

speaker: interviewee
text: With my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: --two brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two brothers and a sister

speaker: interviewer
text: And a sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was with us, but in kibbutz. The kibbutz gave you a room, and she was working in clothing-- washing the clothes and fixing for us for all the kids. That time was start the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they sended away all the parent, the older people, to Haifa in the-- in the schools. And the kids was left fighting. We didn't have [? with ?] what to fight. We fighted [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] Molotov. You know what it is? Would you like to tell me what that is?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is bottles from Coca-Cola. We put it in Molotov, inside, in gas. And inside was from pieces of material. We ripped it and we put it there. We closed it up. If it was leaking, I knew it would burn it out, made a hole. So you needed to trim these very close. If you put it on a tank, on a Jeep, or something, while burning, just fire, you couldn't go out alive from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you made these Molotov cocktails? All the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Underground we made in the kibbutz. We had underground there. We made that. [INAUDIBLE]. And [? Goldie Schweig ?]. And that president would have got killed. He came and learned us how to make that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rabin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. How to make the-- this Molotov. Then, the first tank, what we had-- a Jeep. It was covered like-- made like a tank. And you know, the old-fashioned, we used to washing clothes. The-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The washing board?

speaker: interviewee
text: Board. The for metal. I was in that tank, and in that Jeep. And I had made [INAUDIBLE] made noise on this. Not just one child. We had four or five inside. We made a noise like a tank. And that's the way we took that plates, Degania Alef and Degania Bet. The Arabs was running without pants. They thought that's the tanks coming. And that's we didn't have tanks that time. And the Arab, they came in in-- in Degania Alef. The Arabs went in, and they killed all the cows, all the chickens. They left there just like thousands and thousands of chickens. Thousands of cows. And they were already close to Kinneret. My brother had-- took six [INAUDIBLE] Molotov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meir. That's-- that's the brother-- that brother what I have here, he was-- I find him-- not long ago-- when I came to Canada. My brother, Meir, took six, seven [INAUDIBLE] Molotov. In event the first-- when the first Jeep came with the Arabs to go into our kibbutz, he threw himself with this. And all was burning. And they saw the Jeep burning. They run away. They didn't-- they didn't come into the kibbutz. His body I didn't find. It was just pieces.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lost Meir after the war when you were in Israel already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. To bury him, we couldn't walk. We needed to find pieces from him, what we find. We took a piece of wood. We put it in. And we were crawling like [? soldiers ?] crawling on floor. They-- they were shooting from the mountains, Kinneret and [NON-ENGLISH] Kinneret is in the bottom and [NON-ENGLISH] top. They were shooting. We were crawling, just like soldiers crawling, you know? And we will take him there to bury him. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [SOBBING] I left again alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were alone at that point? You didn't join up with the other people from the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the kibbutz? My brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's who you were close with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was in Haifa, in a-- in a school. All the parents was there, with little babies. At that time, my mother was drinking a-- a coffee or a tea. And [? your ?] glass fall down. And she said, my son got killed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't know how she knew. Two hours later, they find out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother came back to the kibbutz. She had a hard time to be there. The kibbutz gave permission me-- they gave me half a year off. I should go out in the city of Haifa [SIGHS] to go to work, and to help my mom to stand up on their feet. And the kibbutz would send her food every week. But she-- she cannot be in the kibbutz. If she be there, she would die. Every place which she went, and every-- there's Meir. There is there. She couldn't stay. I was having a six month off from my kibbutz. And I was working               in kindergarten.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I-- I bought all the furnitures for my mom. At that time, I was there I met my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to a lady [NON-ENGLISH]. She was a cousin to my mom. She-- she didn't never went to school. She didn't know to write and to read. And she had in America some family, you know. She asked me I should come to write you a letter to the family. And they used to send her money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I saw a picture [INAUDIBLE], nice-looking soldier. You know, very nice-looking man. And I said to you, would you tell me who's that guy? And she said, you want to met him? And I said, I just want to know who this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that time, I wrote you the letter. She write my good letter, I'm going to make sure you going to meet him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I went away to the kibbutz, working there. And a week later, I came to see my mom. I came to see [? you, too, ?] to write you a letter again. And he came with-- that's the only people what he have, family like. Is not-- that's the way it was, Borys, yeah? And that's the way you met?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we met here. We met. From the first moment we met, he didn't want to leave me already. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel at that point in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked him. He was a poor guy. You know, he was in military, and then in the police. My-- my family wanted, I should go out and have a better life, like. And [INAUDIBLE] years, I [INAUDIBLE] to tell. I had lots of boys. Lots of very nice-looking ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were dating when you were in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was dating. I had lots of boyfriends. And from that time we see each other, he want to get married. And I told him, I can't get married. I have to look after my family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he used to come to the kibbutz to visiting. It was a time when I left the kibbutz to stay with my mom. And they find my sister. My older sister came from Pole. And my mother took her in. And that in the house she was pregnant. And I was the bread giver. I gave the food to the table. And he wanted to get married. And I couldn't.  I said, I can't leave my family. I'm the only bread giver in the house. He went-- my husband went to the police and asked them, you have to help me. You have to take my-- my girlfriend's brother-in-law, giving him a job. He couldn't speak Hebrew. He spoke only Polish. And [INAUDIBLE] took him in, and they gave him a job in the police. And now he said, well, you got a job. Now you can get married with me now.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you communicate with him if he spoke Polish and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, he spoke Jewish, too. Jewish is nothing in Israel. You know you couldn't get a job with Jewish or Polish. You needed to speak Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this time, you were-- you had been reunited with your mother, with your brother, Meir, who unfortunately passed away.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the first.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with [? Mosha, ?] your youngest brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the first was Meir.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my mother. Then, [? Mosha. ?] And the last one, the last was my sister, Goldie, came. And when I was here in Canada, I find my brother [? Label. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So many years later you rediscovered another brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know he's alive. We wrote. I wrote. I gave letters to [INAUDIBLE]. She went to Russia lots of times, you know, for the Jewish people. And she came back and she said, they said they don't know even where his bones. They don't know if he's alive. And I knew he's not alive. And my mother was wrote letters and everything. He was in jail. And they didn't know. And that he got married in [? Latvia ?] with a [INAUDIBLE] from [? Latvia ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And he start writing letters. And he find letters whose people told him, you have your mother's looking for you. And he started writing letters. And that's the way we-- they find him. He was in Pole. From Russia, he came to Poland. And he came to Israel. They couldn't get a job. And she was-- she went teaching Hebrew. When he didn't-- a man is not working, [? I don't know why. ?] But he felt so low. He couldn't get a job. Then my mother wrote me. Please, if I can take him. And I was not rich here. That's the first time when we bought a store. I couldn't refuse my mom. I said, I can take him out alone. Let him see if he like it. If he's going to make better, it's OK. Food and room and board would not cost him. And that's the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you took him in.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came. I didn't know he's my brother. I didn't recognize him. He didn't recognize me. And he came to us. And he stayed a year with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll talk more again about all the reunions in a moment.
